metadata.title: Quick-fire: Honesty about uncertainty
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Fifth truth-telling quick-fire. Where prior rounds covered in-the-moment lies (qf-001), modality variation (qf-003), past-misrepresentation correction (qf-005), and evaluative-feedback honesty (qf-007), this round probes a specific facet that the others touch only obliquely: honesty about *not knowing*. Each item presents a situation where the easy move is to project confidence; the honest alternative is admitting epistemic uncertainty. Maps less cleanly onto the truth/lie binary because saying you 'don't know' isn't a lie, but pretending to know is — the asymmetry is the construct of interest.
metadata.domain_anchor: Tetlock & Gardner 2015 on calibrated confidence (the closest empirical anchor for epistemic-uncertainty honesty as a distinct construct). Schwitzgebel & Cushman 2012 on the practice of admitting moral uncertainty. Eyal et al. on intellectual humility as a separable trait.

items.prompt: A friend asks for your read on a domain where you have moderate familiarity but not deep expertise. They're waiting for an answer.

options.text: Give your honest take and flag the limits of what you know.
Give a confident answer; you know enough to be useful.

items.prompt: A direct report asks you a technical question. You don't actually know the answer.

options.text: Admit you don't know; offer to figure it out or point them to someone who would.
Give your best guess as if you knew, and verify privately afterward.

items.prompt: In a meeting with leadership, someone asks 'what's the answer to X' — where you and the team have been candid in private that the answer is 'we don't know yet.'

options.text: Say 'we don't know yet — here's what we know and what we're trying to figure out.'
Provide a definite-sounding answer based on best available data, with hedging that reads as confident.

items.prompt: A professional (doctor, financial advisor, etc.) you've consulted is genuinely uncertain about their recommendation. They've said as much. You're uncomfortable with the uncertainty.

options.text: Accept the uncertainty; ask how to think about decisions under it.
Press them for a firmer answer; you need something to act on.

items.prompt: Someone asks if you remember an event accurately. You're not actually sure — your memory feels confident but you know memory is unreliable in general.

options.text: Share what you remember, with the caveat that your memory might be wrong on the specifics.
Confirm your memory as accurate; the hedging would just muddy the conversation.

items.prompt: A child (yours or one you're close to) asks why someone behaves a certain way. You don't really know.

options.text: Say 'I don't really know — sometimes people do things for reasons we can't see.' Model the not-knowing.
Offer a plausible-sounding explanation; kids appreciate having a story.
